figures.caption: Figure 1: Challenges of Diffusion Language Models and the framework of our unified energy (Uni-E). Uni-E uses a proxy model to capture both dependency and invariance to guide the decoding process.

Table 1: Perplexity of all the methods. The Lower is better. ∗ denotes reproduced results, † taken from [42], ‡ taken from [36]. The best result is in bold and the second best is underlined (except AR).
Table 2: Generative Perplexity under Llama2, Llama3, and GPT2. ↓ means the lower is better. The best results are highlighted with bold. ∗ denotes reproduced results, † taken from [42].

Figure 3: Hyper-parameter and efficiency analysis. From left to right, the first two figures show the influence of candidate size k and sampling window w respectively; the last two figures show the tradeoff between Gen PPL/entropy and wall-clock time. Llama2 is used as the oracle model.
Table 3: Benchmark results. † taken from [39]. ‡ taken from [43]. ∗ means reproduced results.
Table 9: Several benchmark results. Improvements of applying Uni-Energy to their backbone models are shown in green. † taken from [39]. ‡ taken from [43]. ∗ denotes reproduced results.
Figure 5: The invariant decoding case of Uni-E.
Figure 6: The independent decoding case of Uni-E.
Figure 7: The non-invariant decoding case of MDLM.
Figure 8: The non-independent decoding case of MDLM.
